demographics: I am 52 years old. I am female. I am White. I am divorced. I had 8 or more years of college education (doctorate or professional degree). My family income is $170,000 or more. I am a not very strong Republican. I am extremely conservative. My religion is Protestant. I attend religious services more than once a week.

events.event_text: At the age of 28, while serving as a youth pastor, I discovered that a respected church elder had been abusing a teenager from our congregation. I reported it to the authorities, but the aftermath severely tested my faith as our church community struggled with the devastating consequences.

At 35, I was unexpectedly laid off from my job at a prominent Protestant charity I'd dedicated a decade to. Despite our shared faith, the organization's financial struggles and ruthless leadership left me feeling betrayed and questioning my loyalty to the institution.

At 40, I took on a pro bono case defending a young immigrant family facing deportation. Witnessing their strength and unity, despite severe hardships, was profoundly moving and reshaped my perspective on immigration.
At 45, I moved to a diverse urban area for a job promotion, joining a loving interracial church that challenged my preconceived notions and expanded my perspective on race and faith.
At 45, I became the sole caregiver for my mother after her stroke, moving her into my home. It has been challenging, but our doctor and church community have provided unwavering support.

At 42, I was offered a management position at our church's affiliated school, where I've been teaching for years. It was bittersweet, as it meant overseeing budget cuts, but I believed in our mission too much to refuse.

At 48, I finally felt confident enough to challenge the church's outdated policy on female pastoral roles. I gathered fellow congregants, both men and women, and we peacefully picketed the church, drawing local attention. The experience brought us closer and sparked a thoughtful dialogue with the clergy.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person, at 28, exposed a respected church elder's abuse of a teenager, causing a faith crisis as their community grappled with the fallout. At 35, they were abruptly laid off from a decade-long role at a Protestant charity, feeling betrayed by the institution's ruthless leadership. At 40, they took on a pro bono case defending an immigrant family, which reshaped their perspective on immigration.

This person, at 45, moved to a diverse urban area and joined an interracial church, broadening their perspective on race and faith, while also becoming the sole caregiver for their mother post-stroke, receiving support from their doctor and church community. At 42, they accepted a management position at their church's school, overseeing budget cuts despite their reservations, driven by their belief in the mission. At 48, they led a peaceful protest challenging the church's policy on female pastoral roles, fostering dialogue and unity among congregants.

persona_card: "Highly religious yet critical of institutional hypocrisy, this 52-year-old White Protestant woman, a former church insider turned activist, is morally traditional yet open to dialogue, with a nuanced view of racial inequality, and is cautiously trusting of institutions, while maintaining a welcoming stance towards social outgroups."
